frameworks.name: predictive coding
frameworks.one_line: perception as probabilistic inference combining top-down predictions with bottom-up sensory evidence

frameworks.name: hierarchical feedforward models
frameworks.one_line: perception as cascade of increasingly complex feature detectors without top-down inference

core_question: Does current neurophysiological evidence actually distinguish predictive-coding accounts of perception from hierarchical feedforward alternatives?
key_assumptions: predictive coding predicts distinct neural signatures (expectation suppression, prediction-error responses)
feedforward models offer a viable null hypothesis for the same empirical signatures
fMRI/MEG pattern signatures can in principle adjudicate between the two accounts
manipulations: review-level comparison of evidence types: expectation suppression, prediction-error responses, fMRI/MEG decoding signatures
key_findings: empirical signatures invoked for predictive coding are often also compatible with feedforward accounts
expectation suppression and prediction-error responses lack diagnostic specificity as currently measured
fMRI/MEG pattern signatures cited in support of predictive coding are similarly ambiguous
interpretations: current neurophysiological evidence for predictive processing as a model of perception is more limited than commonly claimed
stronger experimental designs are needed to discriminate predictive coding from hierarchical feedforward alternatives
limitations_noted: review focuses on adjudicating evidence rather than proposing new experiments
